title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement codeofconduct@rstudio.com. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

previous_headings: Bigger changes
title: Contributing to bsync
text: Fork package clone onto computer. haven’t done , recommend using usethis::create_from_github("jmgirard/bsync", fork = TRUE). Install development dependencies devtools::install_dev_deps(), make sure package passes R CMD check running devtools::check(). R CMD check doesn’t pass cleanly, ’s good idea ask help continuing. Create Git branch pull request (PR). recommend using usethis::pr_init("brief-description--change"). Make changes, commit git, create PR running usethis::pr_push(), following prompts browser. title PR briefly describe change. body PR contain Fixes #issue-number. user-facing changes, add bullet top NEWS.md (.e. just first header). Follow style described https://style.tidyverse.org/news.html.

what: A Real-World Example: Conversational Head Nods
title: WCC Parameters
text: Imagine studying interpersonal synchrony conversation. video tracking data sampled 30 Hz (30 frames per second), specifically interested Person B nods response Person ’s nods. use suggest_wcc_params(), need define three theoretical values: typical duration event, maximum plausible delay, desired window overlap.
code: library(bsync)  # 30 Hz sampling rate fs <- 30  suggest_wcc_params(   sample_rate = fs,   event_duration_sec = 1,   max_delay_sec = 2,   overlap_pct = 0.5 ) #>  #> ── Suggested WCC Parameters ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> window_size: 120 (4 seconds) #> lag_max: 60 (2 seconds) #> window_increment: 60 (50% overlap) #> lag_increment: 1

previous_headings: A Real-World Example: Conversational Head Nods
title: WCC Parameters
text: first step estimate long single instance target behavior takes complete. example, typical conversational head nod takes approximately 1 second begin, peak, return rest. helper function uses value calculate window_size. intentionally suggests window 4 times duration event (case, 4 seconds, translates 120 frames 30 Hz). 4x multiplier serves two critical purposes: Statistical Stability: Pearson correlations become highly unstable small sample sizes. wider window ensures enough data points reliably calculate correlation coefficient. Contextual Capture: identify two signals correlated, window must large enough capture “quiet” periods movement. window captured peak nod, data appear flat line, making correlation impossible.

previous_headings: A Real-World Example: Conversational Head Nods
title: WCC Parameters
text: Next, must consider biological psychological limits interaction. Person nods, long Person B respond nod considered new, independent action rather reaction? conversational dynamics, response delay 2 seconds often considered completely new initiation. set max_delay_sec = 2. helper function converts directly frames set lag_max (60 frames). important note lag_max mathematically exceed half window_size. shift two signals far apart relative window, overlapping region correlation can calculated becomes dangerously small. helper function automatically cap lag detects imbalance.

previous_headings: A Real-World Example: Conversational Head Nods
title: WCC Parameters
text: overlap_pct parameter dictates temporal resolution final elapsed time axis. controls far window shifts forward calculating correlation landscape. parameter direct trade-computational speed visual smoothness: 0% Overlap: window jumps completely forward (e.g., 4 seconds time). extremely fast compute results blocky, low-resolution plot might skip rapid shifts synchrony. 50% 75% Overlap: balanced choice exploratory data analysis. window slides forward incrementally, providing smooth curve correlation changes keeping processing times low. High Overlap (Increment 1): window moves forward exactly 1 frame time. provides highest possible resolution smoothest peak tracking. bsync optimized C++, many modern machines can handle increment 1 typical datasets, making ideal final publication plots. setting overlap_pct = 0.5, helper function suggests window_increment 60 frames. means 120-frame window shift forward 60 frames step, ensuring continuous coverage conversation.

title: WCC Parameters
text: reviewed suggested parameters, can plug directly core wcc() function run analysis confidence.
code: # Using the suggested parameters from above results <- wcc(   x = df$person_A_nod,   y = df$person_B_nod,   window_size = 120,   lag_max = 60,   window_increment = 60,   lag_increment = 1 )

what: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Cross-Correlation
title: WCC Workflow
text: vignette walks complete Windowed Cross-Correlation (WCC) analysis using bsync package. WCC highly effective quantifying interpersonal synchrony accommodates non-stationary relationships. Unlike global cross-correlation, WCC captures synchronization ebbs flows time. cover data simulation, calculating WCC, surrogate testing statistical significance, optima extraction, visualization. guidance selecting appropriate window sizes lag parameters, please see suggest_wcc_params() vignette.

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Cross-Correlation
title: WCC Workflow
text: demonstrate workflow, simulate realistic interaction two participants (Person Person B) captured 30 Hz. generate smooth continuous data simulate bodily motion add random measurement noise. scenario, Person leads interaction 15 frames (0.5 seconds) start. course 60 seconds, dynamic smoothly transitions Person B leads 15 frames end.
code: library(bsync) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  set.seed(2026)  # Simulation parameters fs <- 30 n_frames <- 1800 # 60 seconds of data  # Generate a smooth base signal using a 30-frame moving average. # This provides enough structure to form a distinct peak within a 90-frame WCC window. raw_noise <- rnorm(n_frames + 300) base_signal <- stats::filter(raw_noise, rep(1/30, 30), circular = TRUE) base_signal <- as.numeric(base_signal)  person_A_raw <- numeric(n_frames) person_B_raw <- numeric(n_frames)  # Continuous lag shift: Person A leads by 15 frames, smoothly transitioning to B leading lag_shifts <- round(seq(15, -15, length.out = n_frames))  for (i in 1:n_frames) {   idx_A <- 150 + i   idx_B <- 150 + i - lag_shifts[i]    # Add slight independent noise to mimic realistic measurement error   person_A_raw[i] <- base_signal[idx_A] + rnorm(1, sd = 0.05)   person_B_raw[i] <- base_signal[idx_B] + rnorm(1, sd = 0.05) }

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Cross-Correlation > 1. Simulating and Preparing Realistic Data
title: WCC Workflow
text: Raw kinematic data almost always requires smoothing analysis. , apply Savitzky-Golay filter iron high-frequency measurement noise. However, polynomial smoothing algorithms require surrounding data calculate accurate average, causes mathematical distortion extreme beginning end recording. use trim_edges() drop edge artifacts, ensuring analysis built strictly stable data. standard rule thumb trim length equal smoothing window.
code: dyad_data <- data.frame(   time = seq(0, by = 1/fs, length.out = n_frames),   person_A_raw = person_A_raw,   person_B_raw = person_B_raw ) |>   mutate(     person_A = smooth_signal(person_A_raw, method = "sgolay", window = 15),     person_B = smooth_signal(person_B_raw, method = "sgolay", window = 15)   ) |>   trim_edges(trim_length = 15)

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Cross-Correlation
title: WCC Workflow
text: data ready, can run primary wcc() function. function slides window across time series calculates cross-correlation various lags within window. use window size 90 frames (3 seconds) maximum lag 45 frames (1.5 seconds). also increment window 30 frames (1 second) allow overlap smooth transitions. wcc() function returns list object class wcc_res containing results data frame, overall Fisher’s Z score, input settings. overall Fisher’s Z score indicates positive global correlation, quantile distribution gives us quick look spread correlation values across entire interaction.
code: wcc_results <- wcc(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   lag_increment = 1 )  # View a summary of the results summary(wcc_results) #>  #> ── Windowed Cross-Correlation Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Windows: 54 #> Total Lags Tested: 91 #> Window Size: 90 #> Max Lag: 45 #> Overall Fisher's Z: 0.5352 #>  #> ── Cross-Correlation Value Distribution ── #>  #>      0%     25%     50%     75%    100%  #> -0.9269 -0.2582  0.0906  0.4734  0.9960 #> ! 1 missing value (NA) detected.

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Cross-Correlation
title: WCC Workflow
text: Time series data inherently autocorrelated. , high cross-correlation values can sometimes occur purely chance. test synchronization observed meaningful, generate null distribution using circular shift method. output gives us empirical p-value calculating proportion surrogate Fisher’s Z scores meet exceed observed Fisher’s Z. observed value higher 100 permutations, empirical p-value reported < 0.01. Note: use n_surrogates = 100 speed exploratory analysis, achieve reliable p-values publication, highly recommended run least 1,000 10,000 permutations.
code: surrogate_results <- wcc_surrogate(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   lag_increment = 1,   n_surrogates = 100 )  print(surrogate_results) #>  #> ── WCC Surrogate Analysis (Pseudo-Synchrony) ─────────────────────────────────── #> Permutations: 100 #> Observed Fisher's Z: 0.5352 #> Average Null Z: 0.4088 #> Empirical p-value: < 0.01 #> ✔ Observed synchrony is significantly greater than chance. #> ℹ Note: 100 permutations may be too few for stable p-values. #> Consider setting `n_surrogates >= 1000` for final reporting.

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Cross-Correlation
title: WCC Workflow
text: heatmap visually informative, often want extract precise lags coordination strongest within time window. pick_optima() function identifies local maximums within WCC grid.
code: # Extract optima using a local search size of 9 wcc_optima_df <- pick_optima(wcc_results, L_size = 9)  print(wcc_optima_df) #>  #> ── WCC Optima Results ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Optima Found: 54 #> Search Method: local #> Search Mode: Peaks (Maxima) #> Local Search Size: 9 #> Strict Monotonic: FALSE #> Showing the first 5 results: #>    i optimum_lag optimum_value #>   46         -10   -0.15961961 #>   76          -2    0.68482860 #>  106          -9    0.01674598 #>  136          -7    0.11919598 #>  166          11    0.97551258 #> # ... with 49 more rows

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Cross-Correlation > 4. Optima Extraction
title: WCC Workflow
text: Choosing right L_size crucial successful local search. L_size must odd integer, defines width neighborhood (frames) algorithm evaluates confirm local maximum. delicate balance strike: L_size small (e.g., 3): algorithm might get trapped tiny noise ripples near lag zero completely miss true interactive response. L_size large (e.g., 45): algorithm might skip fast, immediate interactive response lock onto delayed, mathematically stronger, theoretically irrelevant rhythm. good rule thumb set L_size capture roughly 0.1 0.5 seconds data. 30 Hz, L_size 5 15 frames usually great starting point. pick_optima() computationally lightweight, recommend experimenting several values visually comparing plots.

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Cross-Correlation
title: WCC Workflow
text: Finally, can visualize shifting synchronization landscape. passing time_step argument, axes automatically converted raw frame indices seconds.  resulting plot, clearly see diagonal track optima securely tracing shifting peak lag time, cleanly avoiding edge artifacts thanks preprocessing.
code: plot_optima_overlay(   surface_obj = wcc_results,   optima_df = wcc_optima_df,   time_step = 1 / fs,   show_zero_lag = TRUE )

what: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
title: WDTW Workflow
text: vignette walks complete Windowed Dynamic Time Warping (WDTW) analysis using bsync package. Windowed Cross-Correlation (WCC) excellent capturing linear relationships, WDTW excels finding similarities signals might distorted, stretched, compressed time. important remember WDTW calculates distance metric rather correlation. means lower values indicate stronger synchronization (smaller distance signals).

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
title: WDTW Workflow
text: demonstrate workflow, simulate interaction two participants (Person Person B) captured 30 Hz. generate smooth continuous data simulate bodily motion. scenario, Person leads interaction 15 frames (0.5 seconds) start. course 60 seconds, dynamic smoothly transitions Person B leads 15 frames end.
code: library(bsync)  set.seed(2026)  # Simulation parameters fs <- 30 n_frames <- 1800 # 60 seconds of data  # Generate a smooth base signal using a 30-frame moving average. raw_noise <- rnorm(n_frames + 300) base_signal <- stats::filter(raw_noise, rep(1/30, 30), circular = TRUE) base_signal <- as.numeric(base_signal)  person_A <- numeric(n_frames) person_B <- numeric(n_frames)  # Continuous lag shift: Person A leads by 15 frames, smoothly transitioning to B leading lag_shifts <- round(seq(15, -15, length.out = n_frames))  for (i in 1:n_frames) {   idx_A <- 150 + i   idx_B <- 150 + i - lag_shifts[i]    # Add slight independent noise to mimic realistic measurement error   person_A[i] <- base_signal[idx_A] + rnorm(1, sd = 0.05)   person_B[i] <- base_signal[idx_B] + rnorm(1, sd = 0.05) }  dyad_data <- data.frame(   time = seq(0, by = 1/fs, length.out = n_frames),   person_A = person_A,   person_B = person_B )

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
title: WDTW Workflow
text: data ready, can run primary wdtw() function. function slides window across time series calculates DTW alignment distance various lags within window. strongly recommend leaving scale_data = TRUE (default). DTW distances highly sensitive scale input variables, z-score standardizing data ensures resulting cost matrix driven structural shape behaviors rather arbitrary measurement units. wdtw() function returns list object class wdtw_res containing results data frame, overall mean distance, input settings.
code: wdtw_results <- wdtw(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   lag_increment = 1,   scale_data = TRUE )  # View a summary of the results print(wdtw_results) #>  #> ── Windowed Dynamic Time Warping Analysis ────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Windows: 55 #> Total Lags Tested: 91 #> Window Size: 90 #> Max Lag: 45 #> Overall Mean Distance: 36.6055

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
title: WDTW Workflow
text: full distance matrix informative, often want extract precise lags alignment optimal within time window. correlation (WCC), optimal alignment means highest value (peaks). distance metrics (WDTW), optimal alignment means lowest cost (valleys). pick_optima() function handles seamlessly. providing wdtw_res object, function automatically infers execute global search across window find absolute minimum distance (search_method = "global" find_min = TRUE). returns wdtw_optima data frame containing elapsed time indices, optimal lags, corresponding DTW distance values. console output confirms search mode successfully set “Valleys (Minima)” using “global” search method.
code: # Extract optimal alignment lags (valleys) using the default global search wdtw_optima_df <- pick_optima(wdtw_results)  print(wdtw_optima_df) #>  #> ── WDTW Optima Results ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Optima Found: 55 #> Search Method: global #> Search Mode: Valleys (Minima) #> Showing the first 5 results: #>    i optimum_lag optimum_value #>   46          12      19.21710 #>   76          11      19.71480 #>  106          11      19.48473 #>  136          12      20.30806 #>  166          12      19.16016 #> # ... with 50 more rows

previous_headings: Analyzing Interpersonal Synchrony with Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
title: WDTW Workflow
text: Finally, can visualize shifting synchronization landscape. plot_optima_overlay() function detects working wdtw_res object automatically applies sequential viridis color palette map distances, plotting global minima points top. passing time_step argument, axes automatically converted raw frame indices seconds.  resulting plot, lighter colors represent smaller distances (stronger synchrony). see diagonal track corresponding simulated shift dyad’s interaction. overlaid points map exactly lowest alignment costs, smoothly tracing transition Person leading Person B leading.
code: plot_optima_overlay(   surface_obj = wdtw_results,   optima_df = wdtw_optima_df,   time_step = 1 / fs,   show_zero_lag = TRUE )

title: Authors and Citation
text: Jeffrey Girard. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Girard J (2026). bsync: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses. R package version 0.0.0.9000, https://jmgirard.github.io/bsync/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {bsync: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses},   author = {Jeffrey Girard},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://jmgirard.github.io/bsync/}, }

title: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses
text: goal bsync provide modern, high-efficiency R toolkit analyzing interpersonal behavioral synchrony. traditional cross-correlation assumes association two time series stationary time, many psychological behavioral contexts (interpersonal conversation synchronized movement), lead-lag relationship individuals highly dynamic. bsync allows researchers quantify nonstationary associations using highly optimized windowed cross-correlation (WCC), windowed dynamic time warping (WDTW), optima-extraction algorithms. Furthermore, package provides complete analytical pipeline behavioral time series. includes robust preprocessing functions time-bin aggregation, zero-phase signal smoothing, kinematic velocity/speed calculations, well rigorous hypothesis testing via circular-shift surrogate data generation (pseudo-synchrony testing).

title: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses
text: can install development version bsync GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("jmgirard/bsync")

title: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses
text: following example demonstrates Quick Start WCC pipeline. deeper dive WCC learn Windowed Dynamic Time Warping (WDTW), please see included package vignettes. use included sim_dyad dataset, contains 30 seconds simulated 3D motion tracking data two individuals. simulation, Person begins leading rhythmic movement, synchronize middle, Person B takes lead end. First, load package prepare data. highly recommend smoothing positional data calculating velocities prevent high-frequency noise amplifying skewing cross-correlations. apply Savitzky-Golay filter calculate 1D directional velocity Z-axis. Next, calculate windowed cross-correlations. Calling summary() resulting object provides clean, formatted overview analysis settings distribution correlation values. cross-correlations calculated, extract specific lags represent optimal association within time window. Printing optima object displays extraction metadata alongside first results. Finally, visualize resulting correlation landscape. underlying heatmap represents cross-correlation values lag elapsed time window. overlaid points connecting lines demonstrate algorithm successfully tracking shifting lag optimum association course interaction.
code: library(bsync) library(dplyr)  data("sim_dyad")  # Step 1: Smooth the raw position data and calculate 1D velocity df <- sim_dyad |>   mutate(     z_A_smooth = smooth_signal(z_A, method = "sgolay", window = 5),     z_B_smooth = smooth_signal(z_B, method = "sgolay", window = 5),     vel_A = calc_velocity_1d(time, z_A_smooth, fill_edges = TRUE),     vel_B = calc_velocity_1d(time, z_B_smooth, fill_edges = TRUE)   ) # Step 2: Calculate Windowed Cross-Correlations wcc_results <- wcc(   x = df$vel_A,   y = df$vel_B,   window_size = 150,   lag_max = 75,   window_increment = 25,   lag_increment = 1,   na.rm = TRUE )  # View the summary summary(wcc_results) #>  #> ── Windowed Cross-Correlation Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Windows: 87 #> Total Lags Tested: 151 #> Window Size: 150 #> Max Lag: 75 #> Overall Fisher's Z: 1.065 #>  #> ── Cross-Correlation Value Distribution ── #>  #>      0%     25%     50%     75%    100%  #> -0.9984 -0.6770  0.0410  0.7185  0.9986 #> ! 78 missing values (NA) detected. # Step 3: Extract the Optima (Local Maxima) optima <- pick_optima(   obj = wcc_results,   L_size = 5,   strict_monotonic = FALSE,   search_method = "local" )  # View the optima results optima #>  #> ── WCC Optima Results ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Optima Found: 87 #> Search Method: local #> Search Mode: Peaks (Maxima) #> Local Search Size: 5 #> Strict Monotonic: FALSE #> Showing the first 5 results: #>    i optimum_lag optimum_value #>   76          30     0.9742437 #>  101          30     0.9811713 #>  126          30     0.9839800 #>  151          30     0.9846598 #>  176          29     0.9900110 #> # ... with 82 more rows # Step 4: Plot the correlation landscape and overlay the shifting optima plot_optima_overlay(   surface_obj = wcc_results,   optima_df = optima )

previous_headings: Example Workflow
title: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses
text: Psychological behavioral time series highly autocorrelated, means random noise can sometimes look like genuine interaction. verify observed synchronization statistically significant, can use wcc_surrogate() function. function uses circular shift method misalign two time series, generating null distribution “pseudo-synchrony” preserves natural autocorrelation data breaks dyadic interaction.
code: # Step 5: Run surrogate analysis to calculate an empirical p-value set.seed(2026) surrogate_results <- wcc_surrogate(   x = df$vel_A,   y = df$vel_B,   window_size = 150,   lag_max = 75,   window_increment = 25,   lag_increment = 1,   n_surrogates = 100 )  surrogate_results #>  #> ── WCC Surrogate Analysis (Pseudo-Synchrony) ─────────────────────────────────── #> Permutations: 100 #> Observed Fisher's Z: 1.065 #> Average Null Z: 0.9894 #> Empirical p-value: < 0.01 #> ✔ Observed synchrony is significantly greater than chance. #> ℹ Note: 100 permutations may be too few for stable p-values. #> Consider setting `n_surrogates >= 1000` for final reporting.

title: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses
text: preprocessing pipeline utilizes zero-phase moving averages polynomial filtering techniques originally developed : Savitzky, ., & Golay, M. J. (1964). Smoothing differentiation data simplified least squares procedures. Analytical Chemistry, 36(8), 1627-1639.

what: Aggregate Time Series Data by Time Bins — aggregate_by_time
title: Aggregate Time Series Data by Time Bins — aggregate_by_time
text: Efficiently downsamples time series data calculating mean values within specified time bins. highly recommended high-resolution data (e.g., 30Hz OpenFace output) prior calculating velocity running windowed cross-correlation.

title: Aggregate Time Series Data by Time Bins — aggregate_by_time
code: aggregate_by_time(data, time_var, bin_width, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Aggregate Time Series Data by Time Bins — aggregate_by_time
text: data data frame containing time series data. time_var unquoted name column containing time values. bin_width numeric value specifying width time bins. units time variable (e.g., 0.1 100ms bins). na.rm logical indicating whether remove missing values calculating mean. Default `TRUE`.

title: Aggregate Time Series Data by Time Bins — aggregate_by_time
text: new data frame downsampled time series. time variable   updated represent center bin, non-numeric columns   dropped.

title: bsync: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses — bsync-package
text: comprehensive toolkit analyzing interpersonal behavioral synchrony continuous time series data. Provides highly efficient, C++ backed implementations windowed cross-correlation (WCC) peak-picking algorithms quantify nonstationary lead-lag relationships. Includes complete preprocessing pipeline behavioral data featuring zero-phase smoothing, time-bin aggregation, kinematic velocity speed calculations. Additionally, package supports rigorous null-hypothesis testing circular-shift surrogate data generation (pseudo-synchrony analysis) distinguish genuine interaction spurious correlation.

title: bsync: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses — bsync-package
text: Maintainer: Jeffrey Girard @jmgirard.com Authors: Jeffrey Girard @jmgirard.com

title: Calculate 1D Speed — calc_speed_1d
text: Calculates absolute speed along single axis (e.g., x-axis) using finite difference methods.

title: Calculate 1D Speed — calc_speed_1d
code: calc_speed_1d(   t,   x,   n = 1,   method = c("central", "forward", "backward"),   fill_edges = TRUE )

title: Calculate 1D Speed — calc_speed_1d
text: t numeric vector representing time. x numeric vector coordinates. n integer specifying step size difference calculations. Default 1. method character string specifying method use: "central", "forward", "backward". Default "central". fill_edges logical indicating whether automatically use forward backward differences estimate speeds boundaries `method = "central"`. Default `TRUE`.

title: Calculate 1D Speed — calc_speed_1d
text: numeric vector speeds length input vectors.

what: Find Optimum (Peak or Valley) in Windowed Analyses — pick_optima
title: Find Optimum (Peak or Valley) in Windowed Analyses — pick_optima
text: Find Optimum (Peak Valley) Windowed Analyses

title: Find Optimum (Peak or Valley) in Windowed Analyses — pick_optima
code: pick_optima(   obj,   L_size = NULL,   strict_monotonic = FALSE,   find_min = NULL,   search_method = NULL )

title: Find Optimum (Peak or Valley) in Windowed Analyses — pick_optima
text: obj object class "wcc_res" "wdtw_res". L_size odd integer specifying size local search region. Ignored `search_method = "global"`. Default `NULL`. strict_monotonic Logical indicating whether strictly enforce monotonic flanks around extremum. Ignored `search_method = "global"`. Default FALSE. find_min Logical indicating whether search local minima instead local maxima. `NULL` (default), function automatically searches maxima (`FALSE`) cross-correlation ("wcc_res") minima (`TRUE`) distance metrics ("wdtw_res"). search_method Character string specifying "local" "global" search. "local" searches symmetrically outward lag 0. "global" searches entire window absolute extremum. `NULL`, defaults "local" "wcc_res" "global" "wdtw_res".

title: Find Optimum (Peak or Valley) in Windowed Analyses — pick_optima
text: data frame class "wcc_optima" "wdtw_optima".

title: Simulated Dyadic 3D Positional Data — sim_dyad
text: simulated dataset containing 3D positional coordinates two interacting individuals (Person Person B) 30-second duration. data sampled 80 Hz features shifting lead-lag relationship along z-axis. Specifically, Person initially leads interaction 0.5 seconds, smoothly transitions Person B leading 0.5 seconds. Realistic low-level sensor jitter applied coordinates mimic continuous behavioral measures like motion capture computer vision tracking.

title: Simulated Dyadic 3D Positional Data — sim_dyad
code: sim_dyad

title: Simulated Dyadic 3D Positional Data — sim_dyad
text: data frame 2400 rows 7 variables: time Elapsed time seconds. x_A Simulated x-axis coordinate Person (noise ). y_A Simulated y-axis coordinate Person (noise ). z_A Simulated z-axis coordinate Person (0.5 Hz sine wave + noise). x_B Simulated x-axis coordinate Person B (noise ). y_B Simulated y-axis coordinate Person B (noise ). z_B Simulated z-axis coordinate Person B (0.5 Hz sine wave + shifting lag + noise).

what: Smooth a Time Series Signal — smooth_signal
title: Smooth a Time Series Signal — smooth_signal
text: Applies smoothing filter numeric vector. Smoothing highly recommended prior calculating velocity running windowed cross-correlation (WCC) reduce high-frequency noise prevent spurious correlations.

title: Smooth a Time Series Signal — smooth_signal
code: smooth_signal(   x,   method = c("sgolay", "moving_average", "butterworth"),   window = 5,   sg_order = 3,   bw_cutoff = 0.1,   bw_order = 2 )

title: Smooth a Time Series Signal — smooth_signal
text: x numeric vector representing signal smoothed. method character string specifying smoothing method: "moving_average", "sgolay" (Savitzky-Golay), "butterworth". Default "sgolay". window integer specifying window size (number data points) "moving_average" "sgolay" methods. Must odd number "sgolay". Default 5. sg_order integer specifying polynomial order Savitzky-Golay filter. Must less `window`. Default 3. bw_cutoff numeric value 0 1 specifying normalized cutoff frequency Butterworth filter. Default 0.1. bw_order integer specifying order Butterworth filter. Default 2.

title: Smooth a Time Series Signal — smooth_signal
text: numeric vector containing smoothed signal, length `x`.

title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
text: Calculates principled starting values Windowed Cross-Correlation parameters based sampling rate data theoretical timing behaviors.

title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
code: suggest_wcc_params(   sample_rate,   event_duration_sec = 2,   max_delay_sec = 3,   overlap_pct = 0.5 )

title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
text: sample_rate numeric value indicating sampling rate Hertz (frames per second). event_duration_sec expected duration single behavioral event seconds. Default 2 (typical brief conversational gestures). max_delay_sec maximum plausible reaction time participants seconds. Default 3. overlap_pct desired percentage overlap consecutive time windows. Default 0.5 (50 percent overlap).

title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
text: list recommended parameters ready passed `wcc()`.

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation — wcc
text: Conduct windowed cross-correlation analysis

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation — wcc
code: wcc(   x,   y,   window_size,   lag_max,   window_increment = 1,   lag_increment = 1,   na.rm = TRUE )

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation — wcc
text: x numeric vector containing time series (length `y`). y numeric vector containing time series (length `x`). window_size positive integer indicating size window, .e., number elements window vector. Boker et al. suggest setting window small enough assumption can made little change lead-lag relationships within number samples window small reliability correlation estimate sample reduced. lag_max positive integer indicating maximum lag try `x` `y` windows. Boker et al. recommend selecting greatest interval time separating behavior participant `x` behavior participant `y` considered interest. window_increment positive integer indicating number samples successive changes window `x` vector. Can made larger 1 reduce number rows output matrix. Boker et al. recommend setting window increment long possible, long relation successive rows results matrix lost. (default = `1`) lag_increment positive integer indicating number samples successive changes window `y` vector (thus also interval time separating successive columns results matrix). Boker et al. recommend setting lag increment longest lag increment still results related change successive columns. (default = `1`) na.rm logical indicating whether remove missing values windows calculating windowed cross-correlations. (default = `TRUE`)

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation — wcc
text: list object class "wcc" containing results matrix useful   summaries .

title: Calculate Surrogate Windowed Cross-Correlations — wcc_surrogate
text: Generates null distribution Windowed Cross-Correlation (WCC) values using circular shift method. tests whether observed synchronization significantly greater expected chance given inherent autocorrelation individual time series.

title: Calculate Surrogate Windowed Cross-Correlations — wcc_surrogate
code: wcc_surrogate(   x,   y,   window_size,   lag_max,   window_increment = 1,   lag_increment = 1,   na.rm = TRUE,   n_surrogates = 100 )

title: Calculate Surrogate Windowed Cross-Correlations — wcc_surrogate
text: x numeric vector containing time series. y numeric vector containing time series. window_size positive integer indicating size window. lag_max positive integer indicating maximum lag try. window_increment positive integer indicating window shift increment. Default 1. lag_increment positive integer indicating lag shift increment. Default 1. na.rm logical indicating whether remove missing values. Default `TRUE`. n_surrogates integer specifying number surrogate permutations run. Default 100.

title: Calculate Surrogate Windowed Cross-Correlations — wcc_surrogate
text: list object class "wcc_surr" containing observed Fisher's Z,   distribution surrogate Z scores, empirical p-value, settings.

title: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping — wdtw
text: Conduct windowed dynamic time warping (WDTW) analysis find optimal alignment distance sliding windows two time series.

title: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping — wdtw
code: wdtw(   x,   y,   window_size,   lag_max,   window_increment = 1,   lag_increment = 1,   scale_data = TRUE )

title: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping — wdtw
text: x numeric vector containing time series (length `y`). y numeric vector containing time series (length `x`). window_size positive integer indicating size window. lag_max positive integer indicating maximum lag try. window_increment positive integer indicating window shift increment. (default = `1`) lag_increment positive integer indicating lag shift increment. (default = `1`) scale_data logical indicating whether z-score standardize time series prior calculation. Highly recommended DTW. (default = `TRUE`)

title: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping — wdtw
text: list object class "wdtw_res".
